section.title: Sing to the Lord a New Song
section.sectionRationale: The fourth Hallelujah psalm calls God's people to sing a new song in the assembly of the godly, rejoicing in their Maker and King, and pictures praise itself as strength for the certain, final defeat of evil, an honor given to all His saints.
section.thesis: The LORD delights in His people and adorns the humble with salvation, and calls them to a joyful, wholehearted worship that is itself a weapon against evil, anticipating the new song of the redeemed before the Lamb, Revelation 5:9, and Christ's final victory over every rebellious power.
section.frameNote: Two movements: a call to joyful new praise in the assembly of the godly, verses 1-4; and praise as strength for the certain judgment God's people share in, verses 5-9.

units.label: Sing to the Lord a new song
units.note: Israel rejoices in her Maker and King, who delights in His people

verses.text: {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}! Sing to the LORD a {{shir-chadash|new song}}, His praise in the assembly of the {{chasidim|godly}}.
Let Israel rejoice in their Maker; let the children of Zion rejoice in their King.
Let them praise His name with dancing, and make music to Him with tambourine and harp.
For the LORD takes pleasure in His people; He adorns the afflicted with salvation.

units.label: Let the high praises of God be in their mouths
units.note: praise as strength for God's people, and the certainty of His judgment

verses.text: Let the saints exult in glory; let them shout for joy upon their beds.
May the high praises of God be in their mouths, and a double-edged sword in their hands,
to inflict vengeance on the nations and punishment on the peoples,
to bind their kings with chains and their nobles with shackles of iron,
to execute the judgment written against them. This honor is for all His saints. {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}!

haleluyah.gloss: An imperative call, praise the LORD. It opens and closes each of the last five psalms of the Psalter, framing the whole collection's final movement as one continuous shout of praise.

shir-chadash.gloss: A new song, fresh praise for a fresh work of God. The same phrase anticipates the new song sung to the Lamb by the redeemed of every nation, Revelation 5:9.

chasidim.gloss: The godly ones, or saints, bound to God in covenant loving devotion, chesed. The same word describes the LORD's own people who exult in Him and carry out His judgment.

basic.context: This psalm calls the covenant community to sing a new song, joyful, embodied praise with dancing and instruments, because the LORD delights in His people and adorns the humble with salvation. It then pairs that praise with a picture of God's people executing His judgment on rebellious nations, an honor shared by all His saints.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a hymn of praise that moves into vivid, holy war imagery. Its historical setting envisions Israel as God's instrument of judgment on the nations; its abiding pattern is joyful, wholehearted praise and confident trust that God will finally set every wrong right.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes with haleluyah. It calls for a shir chadash, a new song, fresh praise for a fresh work of God. Those who sing are the chasidim, the godly ones bound to God in covenant loving devotion, who both praise and, in the psalm's imagery, carry out God's judgment.
basic.typology: The new song of verse 1 anticipates the new song sung to the Lamb by the redeemed of every tribe and nation, Revelation 5:9-10. The double-edged sword in the mouths and hands of God's people, verse 6, anticipates the word of God as a two-edged sword, Hebrews 4:12, and Christ Himself striking the nations with the sword from His mouth at His return, Revelation 19:15.
basic.christ: The praise and judgment this psalm holds together are fulfilled in Christ, who is Himself the Lamb who receives the new song of the redeemed, Revelation 5:9, and who alone executes final judgment on the nations with the sword of His mouth, Revelation 19:15, while entrusting His people now with the sword of the Spirit, the word of God, Ephesians 6:17.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who takes pleasure in His people and adorns the humble with salvation, who is worthy of ever fresh, joyful, embodied praise, and who is the final judge of every rebellious nation and king, a judgment His people share in and celebrate.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 5:9-10 pictures the redeemed of every nation singing a new song to the Lamb who ransomed them. Hebrews 4:12 calls the word of God living, active, and sharper than any double-edged sword. Revelation 19:15 shows Christ Himself striking down the nations with the sword from His mouth at His return.
basic.oneStory: God's people are called to ever fresh praise because the LORD delights in them and saves the humble, and that praise is bound up with confident hope in His final justice, fulfilled when Christ, the Lamb who receives the new song of the redeemed, returns to judge the nations and vindicate His saints forever.

context.literary: The fourth of the Psalter's closing Hallelujah psalms, moving from a joyful call to praise in the assembly, verses 1-4, to praise paired with holy war imagery entrusted to God's people, verses 5-9, closing with the same shout that opened it.
context.historical: Likely composed for post-exilic worship, when Israel, having returned to the land, longed for the fullness of God's promised kingdom and the final defeat of hostile nations, drawing on older traditions of Israel as God's instrument of judgment, as in Joshua and Judges.
context.canonical: Anticipates Isaiah 61:6's naming of Israel as priests of the LORD who declare His verdicts, and points toward Revelation 5:9-10 and 19:15, where the redeemed sing a new song to the Lamb and Christ Himself executes the final judgment this psalm envisions.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's covenant people to ever fresh, joyful, embodied praise because the LORD delights in them and saves the humble, and to assure them that His justice against every rebellious power is certain, an honor and hope shared by all His saints.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes ancient Israel's specific vocation as God's instrument of judgment on hostile nations under the old covenant; it prescribes as an abiding pattern joyful, wholehearted praise and confident hope that God will finally judge all evil, now understood through Christ.

debates.question: How should Christians read the call to bind kings with chains and inflict vengeance on the nations, verses 6-8, given the New Testament's different pattern of engagement with unbelieving nations?

views.label: A specific mandate for ancient Israel's covenant wars, not a pattern for the church today
views.case: Israel functioned as a theocratic nation under God's direct command in specific historical conflicts, Joshua 6, Judges 7; the New Testament explicitly teaches that Christ's kingdom does not advance by earthly force, John 18:36, and believers are told to leave vengeance to God, Romans 12:19.

views.label: A pattern that anticipates final eschatological judgment, now carried out through the word rather than the sword
views.case: The New Testament reapplies this same sword imagery to the word of God, Ephesians 6:17, Hebrews 4:12, and reserves literal judgment on the nations for Christ's own return, Revelation 19:15, so the psalm's confidence in God's justice remains, though its means have shifted from Israel's arms to Christ's own word and return.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the church today does not wage literal war for the kingdom; the psalm's enduring truth is that God's justice against evil is certain, now proclaimed through the gospel and finally executed by Christ Himself.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's frame, haleluyah, calls for renewed praise. It summons a shir chadash, a new song, matching a fresh work of God, sung by the chasidim, the godly ones bound to Him in covenant loving devotion, who both exult in worship and, in the psalm's vivid imagery, share in executing His judgment.

typology.type: The new song sung in the assembly of the godly, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the new song sung by the redeemed of every nation to the Lamb who ransomed them, Revelation 5:9-10.

typology.type: The double-edged sword in the mouths and hands of God's people, verse 6
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the word of God as a sharp, living sword, Hebrews 4:12, Ephesians 6:17, and in Christ's own sword-like word executing final judgment at His return, Revelation 19:15.

study.christ: Christ is the Lamb to whom the redeemed of every nation sing the new song this psalm anticipates, Revelation 5:9-10. He is also the one who alone finally executes the judgment this psalm envisions, striking down the nations with the sword of His mouth at His return, Revelation 19:15, while His people now carry, not a literal sword, but the sword of the Spirit, the word of God, Ephesians 6:17.
study.god: God is shown as the one who takes pleasure in His people and adorns the humble with salvation, worthy of fresh, joyful, embodied praise, dancing and instruments alike, and as the certain judge of every rebellious nation and king, a judgment His people share in celebrating even now.

crossReferences.note: Pictures the redeemed of every nation singing a new song to the Lamb who ransomed them, fulfilling this psalm's call to fresh praise.
Calls the word of God living, active, and sharper than any double-edged sword, reapplying this psalm's sword imagery.
Shows Christ striking down the nations with the sword from His mouth at His return, the final fulfillment of this psalm's judgment imagery.

study.oneStory: God's people are called to ever fresh, joyful praise because the LORD delights in them and saves the humble, and that praise is bound up with confident hope in His final justice. Both are fulfilled in Christ, the Lamb who receives the new song of the redeemed and who alone will execute final judgment and vindicate His saints forever.

quiz.q: What are God's people called to sing, verse 1?

options.text: A new song, His praise in the assembly of the godly.
options.note: The psalm opens with a call to fresh, communal praise.

options.text: An old song, repeated exactly as before.
options.note: The psalm specifically calls for a new song, not mere repetition.

options.text: A song of complaint against their enemies.
options.note: The song is praise directed to God, not primarily complaint.

options.text: A private song, sung only in solitude.
options.note: The setting named is the assembly of the godly, a communal gathering.

quiz.q: According to verse 4, in whom does the LORD take pleasure?

options.text: In His people; He adorns the afflicted, or humble, with salvation.
options.note: God's delight rests on His own people, especially the humble whom He saves.

options.text: Only in kings and the powerful.
options.note: The verse specifically names the afflicted, or humble, not the powerful.

options.text: In no one; God takes pleasure in nothing.
options.note: The verse states the opposite, that the LORD takes pleasure in His people.

options.text: Only in angels, not in human beings.
options.note: The verse is explicitly about God's own human people, not angels.

quiz.q: What two things are described together in verse 6?

options.text: The high praises of God in their mouths, and a double-edged sword in their hands.
options.note: Worship and readiness for God's judgment are pictured side by side.

options.text: A crown and a scepter.
options.note: The verse names praise and a sword, not royal regalia.

options.text: Silence and mourning.
options.note: The verse describes vocal praise, not silence.

options.text: A harp and a lyre only.
options.note: Instruments appear earlier, in verse 3; verse 6 pairs praise with a sword image.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament reframe the sword imagery of this psalm?

options.text: As the word of God, a double-edged sword, Hebrews 4:12, and the sword of the Spirit, Ephesians 6:17, with final judgment executed by Christ Himself, Revelation 19:15.
options.note: The New Testament transforms literal warfare imagery into the power of God's word and Christ's own final judgment.

options.text: By commanding believers to take up literal weapons against unbelievers.
options.note: The New Testament explicitly teaches the opposite, that Christ's kingdom does not advance by earthly force, John 18:36.

options.text: By removing all mention of judgment from Christian teaching.
options.note: Judgment remains a real New Testament theme, though entrusted finally to Christ rather than to human armies.

options.text: By applying it only to Israel's ancient enemies, with no ongoing relevance.
options.note: The New Testament reapplies the imagery meaningfully to the word of God and Christ's return, not merely as a closed historical footnote.

quiz.q: What connects this psalm's new song to Revelation?

options.text: Revelation 5:9-10 pictures the redeemed of every nation singing a new song to the Lamb who ransomed them.
options.note: This is a direct thematic and verbal echo of Psalm 149:1's call for a new song.

options.text: Revelation forbids any new songs of praise.
options.note: Revelation celebrates, rather than forbids, the new song of the redeemed.

options.text: The connection is only to Revelation's judgment scenes, not its songs.
options.note: The clearest connection is specifically to Revelation's scenes of song, Revelation 5:9-10.

options.text: The connection exists but is considered minor by most interpreters.
options.note: The new song motif is a widely recognized, significant link between this psalm and Revelation.

questions: God's people are called to sing a new song, verse 1. What fresh reason for praise has God given you recently that deserves fresh words, not just old routines?
The LORD adorns the afflicted, or humble, with salvation, verse 4, and delights in His humble people. Where do you need to receive that truth about yourself rather than striving to earn His favor?
This psalm pairs high praises with a readiness for spiritual battle, verse 6. What would it look like to let praise fuel your resistance to a specific temptation or struggle this week?
The final judgment pictured here is now entrusted to Christ and proclaimed through His word. How does trusting God's ultimate justice free you from taking vengeance into your own hands?
This honor is for all His saints, verse 9. How does knowing you share in Christ's ultimate victory shape your everyday courage?

translationNotes.note: The BSB text places an em dash after new song; it is rendered here with a comma, sing to the LORD a new song, His praise in the assembly of the godly, to match this collection's style without altering the meaning.
Afflicted, also rendered humble, renders a Hebrew word describing those who are lowly or bowed down, whom the LORD adorns with salvation.
Double-edged sword renders a Hebrew phrase for a sword with two mouths, or edges, the same image Hebrews 4:12 applies to the word of God.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 149 (https://biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/149.htm) after the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error. Footnote marker [a] stripped. The BSB source rendered verse 1 with an em dash after 'new song'; this was converted to a comma per the hard rule against em or en dashes. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/truthforlife.org, Keller/gospelinlife.com, Piper/desiringgod.org, Ferguson, Carson/thegospelcoalition.org) was found verified for Psalm 149 this session, so sermon status is set to none rather than fabricated.
